summary: Cantrill argues that unikernels—applications running entirely in privileged kernel mode—are fundamentally unsuitable for production deployment. He systematically dismantles the claimed benefits (performance, security, small size) as either specious or achievable through better means, then delivers his core critique: unikernels are completely undebuggable, lacking even basic tools like ps, netstat, or tcpdump. This reflects what he sees as a 'total lack of operational empathy' from unikernel proponents who hand-wave away production problems with 'just restart it' advice.
key_points: Unikernels' performance claims are undermined by their reliance on hardware virtualization, which carries its own performance tax
Security arguments amount to 'security theater'—unikernels depend on hypervisor protection boundaries while eliminating user-level protection boundaries
The 'small size' argument ignores that hypervisors allocate full DRAM to guests regardless of actual usage, unlike containers
Unikernels require applications to be ported to POSIX-like interfaces or rewritten in specific languages like OCaml
The fundamental problem: unikernels cannot be debugged in production—no ps, no netstat, no tcpdump, no DTrace
This reflects a lack of operational empathy from proponents who suggest simply restarting misbehaving services
money_quotes: Unikernels are entirely undebuggable. There are no processes, so of course there is no ps, no htop, no strace – but there is also no netstat, no tcpdump, no ping!
From a debugging perspective, to say this is primitive understates it: this isn't paleolithic – it is precambrian.
I find the implicit denial of debugging production systems to be galling, and symptomatic of a deeper malaise among unikernel proponents: total lack of operational empathy.
At best, unikernels amount to security theater, and at worst, a security nightmare.

Unikernels are unfit for production not merely as implemented but as conceived: they cannot be understood when they misbehave in production – and by their own assertions, they never will be able to be.
In the fullness of time, I think that unikernels will be most productive as a negative result: they will primarily serve to demonstrate the impracticality of their approach for production systems.
key_insight: Production systems must be debuggable, and any technology that sacrifices observability for theoretical benefits like performance or simplicity is fundamentally unsuitable for real-world deployment.
